QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a data pipeline that ingests streaming clickstream data transforms it and stores it in S3 for model training. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Kinesis Data Streams captures real-time clickstream events. Kinesis Data Firehose buffers and delivers data to S3 in Parquet format with automatic batching and compression. A Glue ETL job can further transform the data for feature engineering. This architecture handles high-throughput streaming ingestion with automatic format conversion and delivery to the data lake.
Option1: Use Kinesis Data Streams for ingestion with Kinesis Data Firehose for S3 delivery in Parquet format
Option2: Use SQS to buffer clickstream events and Lambda to write to S3
Option3: Store clickstream data directly in RDS
Option4: Use EC2 instances to poll for clickstream data

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to prepare a training dataset by joining data from an S3 data lake with data in an RDS MySQL database. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: AWS Glue ETL supports reading from both S3 and JDBC sources (including RDS MySQL) in a single job. The Glue job reads from both sources, performs the join using DynamicFrames or Spark DataFrames, and writes the joined result to S3 in a format suitable for training (Parquet or CSV). Glue handles the distributed processing and data movement automatically.
Option1: Use AWS Glue ETL to read from both S3 and RDS MySQL perform the join and write to S3
Option2: Export RDS data manually and join with S3 data using a script on EC2
Option3: Use Amazon Athena to join S3 and RDS data
Option4: Copy all data to RDS and join there

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to store feature data that will be used for both real-time inference and batch training. Which AWS service provides a unified feature store?
Explanation: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store provides both an online store (for low-latency real-time inference lookups) and an offline store (S3-based for batch training). Features are written once and available in both stores. The online store provides single-digit millisecond reads. The offline store maintains a complete history of feature values for point-in-time correct training datasets.
Option1: Use Amazon SageMaker Feature Store with online and offline stores
Option2: Use DynamoDB for real-time and S3 for batch separately
Option3: Use ElastiCache for all feature storage
Option4: Store features in RDS for both use cases

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to handle a training dataset with significant class imbalance—99% negative and 1% positive examples. Which THREE techniques can address this? (Select THREE)
Explanation: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) generates synthetic positive examples by interpolating between existing positive samples. Undersampling reduces the majority class to balance the dataset. Adjusting class weights in the loss function penalizes misclassification of the minority class more heavily. All three address class imbalance from different angles and can be combined.
Option1: SMOTE to generate synthetic minority class examples
Option2: Random undersampling of the majority class
Option3: Adjusting class weights in the loss function
Option4: Collecting more data from the majority class
Option5: Ignoring the imbalance and training as-is

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to transfer 50 TB of training data from on-premises to S3 as quickly as possible. Their internet connection is 1 Gbps. Which approach is FASTEST?
Explanation: At 1 Gbps, 50 TB takes approximately 5 days to transfer over the network. AWS Snowball Edge (80 TB capacity) can be shipped, loaded, and returned in about 1 week. However, for 50 TB with a 1 Gbps connection, AWS DataSync can optimize the transfer to complete in approximately 5 days using parallel transfers and compression. Snowball is faster for larger datasets or slower connections.
Option1: Use AWS Snowball Edge for the fastest bulk data transfer
Option2: Use the AWS CLI s3 cp command over the internet
Option3: Use AWS Direct Connect which takes weeks to provision
Option4: Compress data and transfer via FTP

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a data pipeline that automatically triggers model retraining when new training data arrives in S3. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: S3 event notifications (via EventBridge) detect new data arrivals. EventBridge triggers a Step Functions workflow that orchestrates the retraining pipeline: data validation (Glue Data Quality), feature engineering (Glue ETL), model training (SageMaker Training), model evaluation (SageMaker Processing), and conditional model deployment (SageMaker Model Registry + Endpoint update). This provides fully automated retraining.
Option1: S3 events triggering Step Functions that orchestrates Glue ETL then SageMaker training and conditional deployment
Option2: Manually trigger retraining when new data is noticed
Option3: Schedule retraining daily regardless of new data
Option4: Use CloudWatch Events to poll S3 for changes

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles missing values in their training dataset. Which TWO approaches are commonly used? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Mean/median/mode imputation replaces missing values with the statistical measure of the non-missing values in that column. This preserves the dataset size while providing reasonable estimates. Dropping rows with missing values is appropriate when the percentage of missing data is small and the missingness is random. The choice depends on the amount and pattern of missing data.
Option1: Impute missing values with mean median or mode of the column
Option2: Drop rows with missing values when the percentage is small and missingness is random
Option3: Replace all missing values with zero regardless of context
Option4: Ignore missing values and train the model as-is

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a data pipeline that processes data incrementally—only new or changed records since the last run. Which AWS Glue feature provides this?
Explanation: Glue Job Bookmarks track which data has been processed in previous job runs. On subsequent runs, the job only processes new or modified files/records, avoiding reprocessing of already-processed data. This significantly reduces processing time and cost for incremental pipelines. Bookmarks work with S3, JDBC, and other Glue-supported data sources.
Option1: Enable AWS Glue Job Bookmarks for incremental data processing
Option2: Process all data on every run and deduplicate
Option3: Use S3 event notifications to track new files manually
Option4: Maintain a custom checkpoint table in DynamoDB

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to convert categorical features (like country city product_category) into numerical representations for model training. Which TWO encoding techniques should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: One-hot encoding creates binary columns for each category value—suitable for nominal categories with low cardinality (few unique values). Label encoding assigns integer values to categories—suitable for ordinal categories (where order matters) or when used with tree-based models that can handle integer-encoded categories. The choice depends on the feature's nature and the model type.
Option1: One-hot encoding for nominal categorical features with low cardinality
Option2: Label encoding for ordinal features or tree-based models
Option3: Store categories as raw strings in the training data
Option4: Convert all categories to their hash values

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that detects and removes duplicate records from their training dataset. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Deduplication using a combination of exact matching (on unique identifiers) and fuzzy matching (on text fields using similarity metrics like Levenshtein distance or cosine similarity) identifies both exact and near-duplicate records. AWS Glue FindMatches ML transform provides managed fuzzy matching that learns to identify duplicates from labeled examples without writing custom matching logic.
Option1: Use AWS Glue FindMatches ML transform for managed deduplication with fuzzy matching
Option2: Manually review all records for duplicates
Option3: Keep all duplicates in the training data
Option4: Remove records randomly to reduce dataset size

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is analyzing a dataset and notices that a feature has a highly skewed distribution with a long right tail. Which transformation can make the distribution more normal?
Explanation: Log transformation compresses the range of large values and expands the range of small values, pulling the right tail toward the center. This makes highly right-skewed distributions more symmetric and approximately normal. Other options include square root transformation (less aggressive) and Box-Cox transformation (which finds the optimal power transformation automatically).
Option1: Apply a log transformation to reduce right skewness
Option2: Apply a square transformation to increase skewness
Option3: Remove all outliers from the dataset
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to identify which features are most important for predicting the target variable. Which THREE techniques can they use? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Correlation analysis measures the linear relationship between each feature and the target—high absolute correlation suggests importance. Feature importance from tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) ranks features by their contribution to predictions. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) identifies which features contribute most to the principal components that explain the most variance. All three provide different perspectives on feature importance.
Option1: Correlation analysis between features and the target variable
Option2: Feature importance scores from tree-based models
Option3: PCA to identify features contributing most to variance
Option4: Alphabetical ordering of feature names

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer discovers that two features in their dataset have a correlation coefficient of 0.95. What problem does this indicate and how should they address it?
Explanation: A correlation of 0.95 indicates multicollinearity—the two features carry nearly identical information. This can cause instability in linear models (coefficients become unreliable) and doesn't add predictive value. The engineer should remove one of the correlated features, combine them into a single feature, or use regularization (L1/L2) which handles multicollinearity by penalizing redundant features.
Option1: Multicollinearity—remove one feature or use regularization to handle redundant information
Option2: The features are perfectly independent and both should be kept
Option3: Both features should be removed entirely
Option4: All of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to reduce the dimensionality of a dataset with 500 features while preserving the most important information. Which technique should they use?
Explanation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) transforms the original features into a smaller set of uncorrelated principal components that capture the maximum variance in the data. The engineer selects the number of components that explain a sufficient percentage of variance (e.g., 95%). SageMaker provides a built-in PCA algorithm. This reduces dimensionality while preserving the most informative signal in the data.
Option1: Use Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensions while preserving maximum variance
Option2: Randomly select 50 features to keep
Option3: Keep all 500 features regardless of redundancy
Option4: Remove features alphabetically

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is analyzing a time series dataset for sales forecasting. Which TWO patterns should they look for during exploratory analysis? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Seasonality refers to regular, predictable patterns that repeat at fixed intervals (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly)—like higher retail sales in December. Trend refers to the long-term direction of the data (increasing, decreasing, or flat). Both are fundamental time series components that forecasting models must capture. Identifying them during EDA informs model selection and feature engineering.
Option1: Seasonality—recurring patterns at fixed intervals
Option2: Trend—long-term directional movement in the data
Option3: Alphabetical ordering of timestamps
Option4: Random noise patterns only

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to detect outliers in their dataset. Which TWO statistical methods can they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The IQR (Interquartile Range) method identifies outliers as values below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR. This is robust to the distribution shape. Z-score identifies outliers as values more than 2-3 standard deviations from the mean—this assumes approximately normal distribution. Both are widely used statistical methods for outlier detection with different assumptions.
Option1: IQR method for distribution-robust outlier detection
Option2: Z-score method for normally distributed data
Option3: Removing the first and last 10% of sorted values
Option4: Treating all values above the mean as outliers

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to visualize the relationship between multiple features simultaneously to identify clusters and patterns. Which visualization technique should they use?
Explanation: A pair plot (scatter plot matrix) shows scatter plots for every pair of features, with histograms on the diagonal. This reveals correlations, clusters, and non-linear relationships between features. For high-dimensional data, t-SNE or UMAP can reduce dimensions to 2D/3D for visualization while preserving local structure, revealing clusters that aren't visible in individual feature plots.
Option1: Use pair plots for pairwise feature relationships and t-SNE/UMAP for high-dimensional cluster visualization
Option2: Use only histograms for each feature independently
Option3: Use pie charts for all features
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is building a text classification model and needs to convert text documents into numerical features. Which TWO techniques should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) converts documents into numerical vectors where each dimension represents a word's importance in the document relative to the corpus. Word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, or BERT embeddings) capture semantic meaning—similar words have similar vectors. TF-IDF is simpler and interpretable; embeddings capture deeper semantic relationships.
Option1: TF-IDF for term importance-based document vectorization
Option2: Word embeddings (Word2Vec GloVe BERT) for semantic representation
Option3: Storing raw text strings as features
Option4: Converting each character to its ASCII value

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to understand the distribution of their target variable for a regression problem. They discover it is heavily right-skewed. Which action should they take before training?
Explanation: Applying a log transformation to the target variable makes its distribution more symmetric, which improves the performance of many regression algorithms (especially linear models) that assume normally distributed residuals. The model predicts log(target), and predictions are exponentiated back to the original scale. This is a common preprocessing step for skewed targets like prices, counts, and durations.
Option1: Apply a log transformation to the target variable to reduce skewness
Option2: Leave the target variable as-is regardless of distribution
Option3: Remove all high-value target observations
Option4: Convert the regression problem to classification

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to scale numerical features so they have similar ranges before training a neural network. Which TWO scaling techniques should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: StandardScaler (z-score normalization) transforms features to have mean=0 and standard deviation=1. This is suitable for features that are approximately normally distributed. MinMaxScaler scales features to a fixed range (typically 0-1). This preserves the shape of the distribution and is suitable for features with bounded ranges. Both prevent features with large ranges from dominating the model.
Option1: StandardScaler for zero-mean unit-variance normalization
Option2: MinMaxScaler for fixed-range scaling between 0 and 1
Option3: Dividing all values by 100
Option4: No scaling is needed for neural networks

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is performing EDA on a dataset with 100 features. They want to quickly identify which features have the most missing values and which have unusual distributions. Which approach is MOST efficient?
Explanation: Pandas profiling (or ydata-profiling) generates a comprehensive EDA report with one line of code. It shows missing value percentages, distributions, correlations, duplicate rows, and alerts for each feature. In SageMaker notebooks, this provides an instant overview of data quality issues across all 100 features without writing individual analysis code for each feature.
Option1: Use an automated EDA tool like pandas profiling to generate a comprehensive report across all features
Option2: Manually examine each of the 100 features one at a time
Option3: Skip EDA and proceed directly to model training
Option4: Use only summary statistics without visualizations

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to determine the optimal number of clusters for a K-means clustering problem. Which TWO methods can they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The elbow method plots the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) against the number of clusters. The optimal K is at the 'elbow' where adding more clusters provides diminishing returns. The silhouette score measures how similar each point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters—higher scores indicate better-defined clusters. Both help determine the optimal K without ground truth labels.
Option1: The elbow method using within-cluster sum of squares
Option2: Silhouette score analysis for cluster quality evaluation
Option3: Always using K=10 regardless of the data
Option4: Using the number of features as K

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to choose an algorithm for a binary classification problem with a large structured dataset. Which algorithm typically provides the best performance with minimal hyperparameter tuning?
Explanation: XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) consistently achieves top performance on structured/tabular data classification tasks. It handles missing values natively, provides feature importance, supports regularization to prevent overfitting, and is relatively robust to hyperparameter choices. SageMaker provides a built-in XGBoost algorithm optimized for distributed training. XGBoost is the go-to algorithm for tabular classification.
Option1: XGBoost for high-performance classification on structured data
Option2: Linear regression
Option3: K-means clustering
Option4: Simple decision tree without boosting

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is training a deep learning model and notices that training loss decreases but validation loss starts increasing after epoch 10. What is happening and how should they address it?
Explanation: This is overfitting—the model is memorizing training data rather than learning generalizable patterns. After epoch 10, the model fits training noise, degrading validation performance. Early stopping monitors validation loss and stops training when it stops improving (with patience for temporary fluctuations). Other mitigations include dropout, data augmentation, regularization, and reducing model complexity.
Option1: The model is overfitting. Use early stopping to halt training when validation loss stops improving
Option2: The model is underfitting and needs more epochs
Option3: The learning rate is too low
Option4: The training data is too large

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to choose between precision and recall for their fraud detection model. False negatives (missed fraud) are much more costly than false positives (flagged legitimate transactions). Which metric should they optimize?
Explanation: When false negatives are costly (missed fraud), recall (sensitivity) should be prioritized. Recall = True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives). High recall means the model catches most fraud cases, even at the cost of some false positives. The F-beta score with beta > 1 (like F2) weights recall more heavily than precision, providing a balanced metric that favors recall.
Option1: Optimize for recall to minimize missed fraud cases (false negatives)
Option2: Optimize for precision to minimize false positives
Option3: Optimize for accuracy only
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to train a model on a dataset that is too large to fit in memory on a single machine. Which SageMaker training approach should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker distributed training supports data parallelism (splitting data across multiple instances, each training on a subset) and model parallelism (splitting the model across instances when the model itself is too large). For large datasets, data parallelism with the SageMaker data parallelism library distributes training across multiple GPU instances, each processing a portion of the data with synchronized gradient updates.
Option1: Use SageMaker distributed training with data parallelism across multiple instances
Option2: Reduce the dataset to fit in memory on a single instance
Option3: Use a larger single instance with more memory
Option4: Train on a random sample of the data

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to find the optimal hyperparameters for their XGBoost model. Which SageMaker feature automates this process?
Explanation: SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (Hyperparameter Optimization) runs multiple training jobs with different hyperparameter combinations. It supports random search, Bayesian optimization (which learns from previous results to choose better combinations), and grid search. The engineer defines the hyperparameter ranges, the objective metric, and the maximum number of training jobs. SageMaker finds the best combination automatically.
Option1: Use SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning with Bayesian optimization
Option2: Manually try different hyperparameter values one at a time
Option3: Use the default hyperparameters without tuning
Option4: Use grid search on a single instance

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to choose a loss function for a multi-class classification problem with 10 classes. Which loss function should they use?
Explanation: Categorical cross-entropy (or sparse categorical cross-entropy for integer labels) is the standard loss function for multi-class classification. It measures the difference between the predicted probability distribution and the true class distribution. The model outputs a probability for each class via softmax activation, and cross-entropy penalizes confident wrong predictions more heavily than uncertain ones.
Option1: Categorical cross-entropy loss with softmax activation
Option2: Mean squared error loss
Option3: Binary cross-entropy loss
Option4: Hinge loss

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is training a neural network and the loss is not decreasing. The model seems stuck. Which THREE adjustments should they try? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Adjusting the learning rate (often reducing it or using a learning rate scheduler) helps the optimizer find the loss minimum. Different optimizers (Adam, SGD with momentum, AdaGrad) have different convergence properties—Adam is often a good default. Checking for data issues (incorrect labels, preprocessing errors, data leakage) ensures the model is learning from valid data. All three address common causes of training stagnation.
Option1: Adjust the learning rate or use a learning rate scheduler
Option2: Try a different optimizer like Adam
Option3: Check for data issues like incorrect labels or preprocessing errors
Option4: Increase the batch size to the maximum possible

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that predicts a continuous numerical value (house prices). Which algorithm should they use?
Explanation: XGBoost regression is highly effective for structured data regression tasks. It handles non-linear relationships, feature interactions, and missing values natively. SageMaker's built-in XGBoost supports regression by setting the objective to reg:squarederror. For simpler relationships, linear regression may suffice. For complex patterns, deep learning regression models can capture non-linear relationships.
Option1: Use XGBoost regression for structured data or linear regression for simpler relationships
Option2: Use K-means clustering for price prediction
Option3: Use a classification algorithm with price ranges
Option4: Use PCA for regression

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement transfer learning for an image classification task with a small dataset (1000 images). Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Transfer learning uses a pre-trained model (like ResNet, VGG, or EfficientNet trained on ImageNet) as a starting point. The engineer freezes the pre-trained layers (which have learned general image features) and replaces the final classification layer with one matching their number of classes. Fine-tuning on the small dataset adapts the model to the specific task. This requires far less data than training from scratch.
Option1: Use a pre-trained image model and fine-tune the final layers on the small dataset
Option2: Train a deep CNN from scratch on 1000 images
Option3: Use K-means clustering on raw pixel values
Option4: Use linear regression on image features

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to evaluate a regression model. Which TWO metrics should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) measures the average magnitude of prediction errors in the same units as the target variable. It penalizes large errors more heavily due to squaring. R-squared (coefficient of determination) measures the proportion of variance in the target explained by the model (0 to 1, higher is better). Both provide complementary views of regression model performance.
Option1: RMSE for average prediction error magnitude
Option2: R-squared for proportion of variance explained
Option3: Accuracy for regression evaluation
Option4: F1 score for regression evaluation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that detects anomalies in network traffic data. The dataset has no labeled anomalies. Which algorithm should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker's built-in Random Cut Forest (RCF) algorithm is designed for unsupervised anomaly detection. It assigns an anomaly score to each data point—higher scores indicate more anomalous observations. RCF works well with high-dimensional data and doesn't require labeled anomalies for training. It learns the normal data distribution and identifies points that deviate significantly from it.
Option1: Use SageMaker Random Cut Forest for unsupervised anomaly detection
Option2: Use XGBoost classification without labels
Option3: Use linear regression for anomaly detection
Option4: Use K-means with K=2 for normal vs anomaly

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a recommendation system that suggests products to users based on their purchase history and similar users' behavior. Which algorithm should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker's built-in Factorization Machines algorithm is designed for recommendation systems. It models interactions between users and items using matrix factorization, capturing latent factors that explain user preferences. For more advanced recommendations, Amazon Personalize provides a fully managed service that handles data ingestion, model training, and real-time recommendation serving without ML expertise.
Option1: Use SageMaker Factorization Machines or Amazon Personalize for recommendation systems
Option2: Use linear regression for recommendations
Option3: Use K-means clustering for product recommendations
Option4: Use PCA for recommendation generation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer is training a model and wants to prevent overfitting. Which THREE regularization techniques can they use? (Select THREE)
Explanation: L1 regularization (Lasso) adds the absolute value of weights to the loss, driving some weights to exactly zero (feature selection). L2 regularization (Ridge) adds the squared weights to the loss, shrinking all weights toward zero (weight decay). Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training, preventing co-adaptation. All three reduce overfitting by constraining model complexity.
Option1: L1 regularization (Lasso) for feature selection
Option2: L2 regularization (Ridge) for weight decay
Option3: Dropout for neural network regularization
Option4: Increasing model complexity

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a natural language processing model that classifies customer support tickets into categories. Which approach provides the best accuracy with minimal training data?
Explanation: Fine-tuning a pre-trained language model (like BERT, DistilBERT, or a model from Hugging Face) on the labeled ticket data provides excellent accuracy even with limited training data. The pre-trained model has already learned language understanding from massive text corpora. Fine-tuning adapts this knowledge to the specific classification task. SageMaker supports Hugging Face models natively.
Option1: Fine-tune a pre-trained language model like BERT on the labeled ticket data
Option2: Train a bag-of-words model from scratch
Option3: Use simple keyword matching rules
Option4: Use K-means clustering on raw text

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to choose between a simple model (logistic regression) and a complex model (deep neural network) for a classification task with 10000 training examples. Which factors should guide their decision? (Select TWO)
Explanation: If the relationship between features and target is approximately linear, logistic regression will perform well and is more interpretable. If the relationship is highly non-linear with complex interactions, a deep neural network can capture these patterns but needs more data and compute. The amount of training data matters—10000 examples may be sufficient for logistic regression but might underfit a large neural network.
Option1: Whether the relationship between features and target is linear or non-linear
Option2: Whether interpretability is required for the use case
Option3: Which model has more parameters
Option4: Which model was published more recently

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement cross-validation to get a reliable estimate of model performance. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: K-fold cross-validation splits the data into K equal folds. The model is trained K times, each time using K-1 folds for training and 1 fold for validation. The final performance estimate is the average across all K folds. This provides a more reliable estimate than a single train/test split because every data point is used for both training and validation. K=5 or K=10 is standard.
Option1: Use K-fold cross-validation with K=5 or K=10 for reliable performance estimation
Option2: Use a single 50/50 train/test split
Option3: Train on all data without any validation
Option4: Use only the first 10% of data for validation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a time series forecasting model for demand prediction. Which AWS service provides managed time series forecasting?
Explanation: Amazon Forecast uses deep learning and statistical algorithms to generate accurate time series forecasts. It automatically selects the best algorithm for the data, handles missing values, and produces probabilistic forecasts with confidence intervals. Forecast supports related time series (like promotions, holidays) and metadata (like product category) for improved accuracy. No ML expertise is required.
Option1: Use Amazon Forecast for managed time series forecasting
Option2: Use linear regression on time features
Option3: Use K-means clustering for forecasting
Option4: Use Amazon Rekognition for demand prediction

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build an object detection model that identifies and locates products on store shelves in images. Which SageMaker built-in algorithm should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker's built-in Object Detection algorithm (based on SSD - Single Shot MultiBox Detector) detects and localizes multiple objects in images. It outputs bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores for each detected object. The algorithm supports transfer learning from pre-trained models and can be trained on custom datasets with bounding box annotations.
Option1: Use SageMaker built-in Object Detection algorithm
Option2: Use SageMaker Image Classification (which only classifies whole images)
Option3: Use SageMaker XGBoost on image pixels
Option4: Use Amazon Textract for object detection

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that generates text summaries of long documents. Which type of model should they use?
Explanation: Sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models with attention mechanisms (like Transformer-based models) are designed for text generation tasks including summarization. Pre-trained models like BART, T5, or GPT can be fine-tuned for summarization. SageMaker supports Hugging Face models for text generation. Amazon Bedrock also provides foundation models capable of summarization without fine-tuning.
Option1: Use a pre-trained seq2seq model like BART or T5 fine-tuned for summarization
Option2: Use XGBoost for text summarization
Option3: Use K-means clustering to extract key sentences
Option4: Use PCA to reduce document dimensions

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to evaluate a binary classification model. The dataset is imbalanced (95% negative 5% positive). Which metric is MOST appropriate?
Explanation: The Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) is the most appropriate metric for imbalanced binary classification. Unlike accuracy (which would be 95% by always predicting negative) or AUC-ROC (which can be misleadingly high with imbalanced data), AUPRC focuses on the model's performance on the minority class. A high AUPRC means the model correctly identifies positive cases with high precision.
Option1: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)
Option2: Accuracy
Option3: AUC-ROC only
Option4: Mean Squared Error

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a model that can handle both numerical and categorical features without extensive preprocessing. Which algorithm handles mixed feature types natively?
Explanation: XGBoost (and other gradient boosting algorithms like LightGBM and CatBoost) handle mixed feature types effectively. Tree-based algorithms naturally handle categorical features (through splits) and numerical features (through threshold comparisons). XGBoost also handles missing values natively by learning the optimal direction for missing values at each split. This reduces preprocessing requirements significantly.
Option1: XGBoost which handles mixed feature types and missing values natively
Option2: Linear regression which requires all numerical inputs
Option3: Neural networks which require all numerical inputs
Option4: K-means which requires all numerical inputs

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to reduce the training time of their deep learning model without significantly impacting accuracy. Which TWO techniques should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Mixed precision training uses both 16-bit and 32-bit floating-point types during training. Forward passes use 16-bit (faster, less memory) while critical operations use 32-bit (maintaining accuracy). This can reduce training time by 2-3x on supported GPUs. Learning rate scheduling (like warm-up + cosine decay) helps the model converge faster by using larger learning rates when safe and smaller rates near convergence.
Option1: Use mixed precision training for faster computation on GPUs
Option2: Implement learning rate scheduling for faster convergence
Option3: Reduce the dataset size by 90%
Option4: Use CPU instead of GPU for training

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that segments customers into groups based on purchasing behavior without predefined labels. Which algorithm should they use?
Explanation: K-means clustering is an unsupervised algorithm that groups data points into K clusters based on feature similarity. For customer segmentation, features might include purchase frequency, average order value, recency, and product categories. SageMaker provides a built-in K-means algorithm optimized for large datasets. The engineer uses the elbow method or silhouette score to determine the optimal number of clusters.
Option1: Use SageMaker built-in K-means clustering for unsupervised customer segmentation
Option2: Use XGBoost classification without labels
Option3: Use linear regression for segmentation
Option4: Use Amazon Forecast for customer grouping

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to deploy a trained model for real-time inference with automatic scaling based on traffic. Which SageMaker feature should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker real-time endpoints host models for synchronous inference with configurable auto-scaling. Application Auto Scaling adjusts the number of instances based on metrics like InvocationsPerInstance or CPUUtilization. The endpoint provides a REST API that returns predictions in milliseconds. SageMaker handles model loading, health checks, and traffic distribution across instances automatically.
Option1: Deploy to a SageMaker real-time endpoint with auto-scaling based on invocation metrics
Option2: Run inference on a single EC2 instance without scaling
Option3: Use Lambda for all real-time inference
Option4: Process all requests in batch mode

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to deploy a model that processes large batches of data (millions of records) for scoring. Real-time latency is not required. Which SageMaker feature should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker Batch Transform processes large datasets by spinning up inference instances, processing all records, and writing results to S3. It automatically distributes the workload across multiple instances for parallel processing. Batch Transform is cost-effective for large-scale scoring because instances are only running during processing—no idle endpoint costs. It supports any model hosted on SageMaker.
Option1: Use SageMaker Batch Transform for cost-effective large-scale batch inference
Option2: Use a real-time endpoint and send records one at a time
Option3: Process records manually in a notebook
Option4: Use Lambda for batch processing

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that monitors their deployed model for data drift—detecting when the input data distribution changes from what the model was trained on. Which SageMaker feature provides this?
Explanation: SageMaker Model Monitor continuously monitors deployed models for data quality drift, model quality drift, bias drift, and feature attribution drift. It compares incoming inference data against a baseline (typically the training data statistics). When drift is detected (e.g., a feature's mean shifts significantly), Model Monitor generates alerts via CloudWatch. This enables proactive model retraining before performance degrades.
Option1: Use SageMaker Model Monitor to detect data drift and trigger alerts
Option2: Manually compare input data distributions monthly
Option3: Assume the model never needs updating
Option4: Use CloudWatch Logs for drift detection

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a CI/CD pipeline for their ML models. Which SageMaker feature provides managed ML pipelines?
Explanation: SageMaker Pipelines provides a purpose-built CI/CD service for ML. It defines ML workflows as DAGs with steps for data processing, training, evaluation, and deployment. Pipelines integrates with SageMaker Model Registry for model versioning and approval workflows. Pipeline executions are tracked with full lineage—which data, code, and parameters produced each model. This provides reproducible, automated ML workflows.
Option1: Use SageMaker Pipelines for managed ML CI/CD with Model Registry for versioning
Option2: Use CodePipeline without any ML-specific features
Option3: Deploy models manually from notebooks
Option4: Use Step Functions for all ML pipeline orchestration

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to deploy multiple models behind a single endpoint to perform A/B testing. Which SageMaker feature supports this?
Explanation: SageMaker endpoint production variants allow multiple model versions to be deployed behind a single endpoint with configurable traffic distribution. For A/B testing, the engineer deploys the current model (variant A, 90% traffic) and the new model (variant B, 10% traffic). SageMaker routes requests according to the weights and provides per-variant metrics for comparison.
Option1: Use SageMaker production variants to deploy multiple models with weighted traffic distribution
Option2: Deploy separate endpoints for each model version
Option3: Use a single model and switch versions manually
Option4: Use API Gateway for traffic splitting

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model is retrained automatically when Model Monitor detects data drift. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Model Monitor publishes drift detection results to CloudWatch. A CloudWatch Alarm triggers when drift exceeds a threshold. EventBridge routes the alarm to a Step Functions workflow (or SageMaker Pipeline) that orchestrates retraining: fetch new data, preprocess, train, evaluate, and conditionally deploy if the new model outperforms the current one. This provides fully automated drift-triggered retraining.
Option1: Model Monitor drift detection triggering CloudWatch Alarm then EventBridge then SageMaker Pipeline for automated retraining
Option2: Manually retrain when drift is noticed
Option3: Schedule retraining monthly regardless of drift
Option4: Ignore drift and keep the current model

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to deploy a model for inference with the lowest possible cost for sporadic traffic (a few requests per hour). Which SageMaker deployment option should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker Serverless Inference automatically provisions compute resources on demand and scales to zero when there are no requests. You pay only for the compute time used during inference—no charges when idle. For sporadic traffic (a few requests per hour), serverless inference is significantly cheaper than maintaining a real-time endpoint that runs 24/7. Cold starts add some latency for the first request after idle.
Option1: Use SageMaker Serverless Inference for pay-per-use pricing with automatic scale-to-zero
Option2: Use a real-time endpoint running 24/7
Option3: Use Batch Transform for each request
Option4: Deploy on a large EC2 instance

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model training is reproducible—the same data and code always produce the same model. Which THREE practices ensure reproducibility? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Setting random seeds ensures that random operations (weight initialization, data shuffling, dropout) produce the same results. Version-controlling training code in Git ensures the exact code used for each model is tracked. Tracking data versions (using SageMaker Experiments or MLflow) ensures the exact training data for each model is recorded. All three are essential for ML reproducibility.
Option1: Set random seeds for all random operations
Option2: Version control training code in Git
Option3: Track data versions used for each training run
Option4: Use different random seeds each time for variety

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model endpoint can handle traffic spikes without dropping requests. Which TWO configurations should they set? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Auto-scaling with target tracking on InvocationsPerInstance automatically adds instances when traffic increases and removes them when traffic decreases. Setting a minimum instance count of 2 or more ensures the endpoint can handle initial traffic without cold-start delays. Together these provide responsive scaling with a baseline capacity that prevents request drops during traffic spikes.
Option1: Configure auto-scaling with target tracking on InvocationsPerInstance
Option2: Set a minimum instance count of at least 2 for baseline capacity
Option3: Use a single instance with no auto-scaling
Option4: Disable health checks to prevent instance replacement

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to track experiments across multiple training runs comparing different algorithms hyperparameters and datasets. Which SageMaker feature provides this?
Explanation: SageMaker Experiments tracks and organizes ML experiments. Each experiment contains multiple trials (training runs), and each trial records parameters, metrics, artifacts, and metadata. The engineer can compare trials side-by-side to identify the best-performing configuration. Experiments integrates with SageMaker Studio for visualization and with Model Registry for promoting the best model to production.
Option1: Use SageMaker Experiments to track and compare training runs across algorithms and hyperparameters
Option2: Record experiment results in a spreadsheet
Option3: Rely on memory to track experiment results
Option4: Use CloudWatch Logs for experiment tracking

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to deploy a model that requires a custom inference container with specific dependencies not available in SageMaker's built-in containers. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker supports custom Docker containers for inference. The engineer builds a Docker image with the required dependencies, implements the model_fn, input_fn, predict_fn, and output_fn functions (or uses a web server like Flask/FastAPI), pushes the image to ECR, and references it in the SageMaker Model configuration. This provides full control over the inference environment.
Option1: Build a custom Docker inference container push to ECR and deploy to a SageMaker endpoint
Option2: Install dependencies at runtime in the built-in container
Option3: Use Lambda for custom inference environments
Option4: Modify the SageMaker built-in container directly

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model is deployed to edge devices for inference without internet connectivity. Which AWS service supports this?
Explanation: SageMaker Neo compiles models for specific hardware targets (ARM, x86, GPU, edge devices) optimizing performance. SageMaker Edge Manager deploys and manages models on edge devices, providing model monitoring and data capture without requiring internet connectivity. The compiled model runs locally on the device, and data is synced when connectivity is available.
Option1: Use SageMaker Neo for model compilation and Edge Manager for edge deployment and management
Option2: Deploy a SageMaker endpoint on each edge device
Option3: Require constant internet connectivity for inference
Option4: Use Lambda@Edge for edge inference

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model training costs are minimized for a long-running training job (24+ hours). Which SageMaker feature reduces training costs?
Explanation: SageMaker Managed Spot Training uses EC2 Spot Instances for training, providing up to 90% cost savings compared to On-Demand instances. SageMaker handles Spot interruptions by checkpointing training progress to S3 and resuming from the checkpoint when capacity is available. For long-running jobs, the cost savings are substantial. The engineer configures checkpointing and a maximum wait time.
Option1: Use SageMaker Managed Spot Training with checkpointing for up to 90% cost savings
Option2: Use the largest On-Demand instance available
Option3: Train on a smaller dataset to reduce time
Option4: Use CPU instances instead of GPU

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model's predictions are explainable—users can understand why the model made a specific prediction. Which SageMaker feature provides this?
Explanation: SageMaker Clarify provides model explainability using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values. For each prediction, Clarify shows the contribution of each feature to the prediction—which features pushed the prediction higher or lower. This is essential for regulated industries (finance, healthcare) where decisions must be explainable. Clarify also detects bias in training data and model predictions.
Option1: Use SageMaker Clarify for SHAP-based model explainability and bias detection
Option2: Use CloudWatch for model explainability
Option3: Assume all model predictions are self-explanatory
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model is versioned and requires approval before being deployed to production. Which SageMaker feature provides this governance?
Explanation: SageMaker Model Registry provides model versioning, metadata tracking, and approval workflows. Each model version is registered with metrics, parameters, and artifacts. Model versions have approval statuses (PendingManualApproval, Approved, Rejected). Only approved models can be deployed to production endpoints. This provides governance and traceability for the model deployment process.
Option1: Use SageMaker Model Registry with approval workflows for governed model deployment
Option2: Deploy models directly from training without any registry
Option3: Use S3 versioning for model governance
Option4: Track models in a spreadsheet

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles data labeling for their training dataset. They have 100000 images that need bounding box annotations. Which AWS service provides managed data labeling?
Explanation: SageMaker Ground Truth provides managed data labeling with built-in labeling workflows for images (bounding boxes, segmentation, classification), text (NER, classification, sentiment), and other data types. It supports human labelers (Amazon Mechanical Turk, private workforce, or third-party vendors) and active learning—where a model pre-labels data and humans verify, reducing labeling cost by up to 70%.
Option1: Use SageMaker Ground Truth with active learning for cost-effective managed data labeling
Option2: Label all 100000 images manually without any tooling
Option3: Skip labeling and use unsupervised learning
Option4: Use Amazon Rekognition to label all images

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that creates point-in-time correct training datasets from their feature store. Why is point-in-time correctness important?
Explanation: Point-in-time correctness ensures that training data only includes feature values that were available at the time of each training example. Without it, future information leaks into training data (data leakage), causing the model to appear more accurate during training than it will be in production. SageMaker Feature Store's offline store maintains feature value history, enabling point-in-time correct dataset creation.
Option1: Point-in-time correctness prevents data leakage by ensuring training data only includes features available at prediction time
Option2: Point-in-time correctness is not important for model training
Option3: It only matters for real-time inference not training
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to determine if their dataset has data leakage—features that would not be available at prediction time. Which approach helps detect this?
Explanation: Suspiciously high model performance (near-perfect accuracy) on the validation set is a strong indicator of data leakage. The engineer should examine each feature and ask: 'Would this feature be available at the time of prediction in production?' Features derived from the target variable, future information, or post-event data are common sources of leakage. Removing these features and retraining reveals the true model performance.
Option1: Check for suspiciously high validation performance and verify each feature would be available at prediction time
Option2: High validation accuracy always indicates a good model
Option3: Data leakage only occurs in production not during training
Option4: All of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to handle a dataset where some features have values on very different scales (age: 0-100 income: 0-1000000). Why is feature scaling important and when is it NOT needed?
Explanation: Feature scaling is important for distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM, K-means) and gradient-based algorithms (neural networks, logistic regression) because features with larger scales dominate the distance calculations or gradient updates. Tree-based algorithms (XGBoost, Random Forest) are NOT affected by feature scaling because they make decisions based on feature value thresholds, not distances or gradients.
Option1: Scaling is needed for distance-based and gradient-based algorithms but NOT needed for tree-based algorithms
Option2: Scaling is always required for all algorithms
Option3: Scaling is never needed for any algorithm
Option4: Scaling only matters for categorical features

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that generates realistic synthetic images. Which type of model should they use?
Explanation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) consist of two neural networks—a generator that creates synthetic images and a discriminator that distinguishes real from fake. They train adversarially: the generator improves at creating realistic images while the discriminator improves at detecting fakes. When training converges, the generator produces images indistinguishable from real ones. Diffusion models are a newer alternative.
Option1: Use Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) or diffusion models for realistic image generation
Option2: Use XGBoost for image generation
Option3: Use K-means clustering for image generation
Option4: Use PCA for image generation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that performs named entity recognition (NER) on text—identifying and classifying entities like person names organizations and locations. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Fine-tuning a pre-trained language model (like BERT) for token classification (NER) provides state-of-the-art accuracy. Each token is classified as an entity type (PERSON, ORG, LOC) or non-entity using the BIO tagging scheme. SageMaker supports Hugging Face models for NER. Amazon Comprehend also provides managed NER without custom model training for common entity types.
Option1: Fine-tune a pre-trained BERT model for NER or use Amazon Comprehend for managed entity recognition
Option2: Use regex patterns for entity extraction
Option3: Use K-means clustering on word embeddings
Option4: Use linear regression for NER

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model inference is optimized for the lowest possible latency. Which TWO SageMaker features help with this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: SageMaker Neo compiles models for specific hardware targets, optimizing the computational graph for faster inference (up to 2x speedup). SageMaker Inference Recommender benchmarks the model across different instance types and configurations to find the optimal instance type that provides the best latency-cost tradeoff. Together they optimize both the model and the infrastructure for minimum latency.
Option1: Use SageMaker Neo for model compilation and hardware-specific optimization
Option2: Use SageMaker Inference Recommender to find the optimal instance type
Option3: Use the largest available instance regardless of the model
Option4: Use CPU instances for all deep learning inference

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model can be rolled back to a previous version if the new version performs poorly in production. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: SageMaker Model Registry maintains versioned models with metadata and approval status. If a new model version performs poorly, the engineer updates the endpoint to point to the previous approved model version in the registry. SageMaker endpoint updates with blue/green deployment ensure zero-downtime rollback. Model Monitor detects performance degradation that triggers the rollback decision.
Option1: Use SageMaker Model Registry for versioning and endpoint update for zero-downtime rollback to previous versions
Option2: Retrain the model from scratch
Option3: Keep only the latest model version
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model training automatically selects the best algorithm from multiple candidates. Which SageMaker feature provides this?
Explanation: SageMaker Autopilot automatically explores multiple algorithms and hyperparameter configurations for a given dataset and problem type. It generates multiple candidate models, evaluates them, and ranks them by performance. The engineer can deploy the best model or inspect the generated notebooks to understand the approach. Autopilot supports classification, regression, and time series forecasting.
Option1: Use SageMaker Autopilot for automatic algorithm selection and model generation
Option2: Manually try each algorithm one at a time
Option3: Always use XGBoost regardless of the problem
Option4: Use the first algorithm that produces any result

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles streaming feature computation for real-time inference. Features like 'number of transactions in the last hour' must be computed in real-time. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams ingests real-time transaction events. Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink computes streaming aggregations (count, sum, average over sliding windows). Computed features are written to SageMaker Feature Store's online store for low-latency retrieval during inference. The inference endpoint reads features from the online store to make predictions with up-to-date feature values.
Option1: Kinesis for event ingestion with Flink for streaming feature computation stored in SageMaker Feature Store online store
Option2: Compute features in batch daily and use stale values for real-time inference
Option3: Compute features in the inference endpoint for each request
Option4: Use static features that never change

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to analyze the relationship between a categorical feature and a continuous target variable. Which statistical test should they use?
Explanation: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests whether the means of the target variable differ significantly across the categories of the feature. A significant ANOVA result indicates the categorical feature has a meaningful relationship with the target. For two categories, a t-test is equivalent. For non-normal distributions, the Kruskal-Wallis test is a non-parametric alternative.
Option1: Use ANOVA to test if the target variable means differ significantly across categories
Option2: Use Pearson correlation for categorical features
Option3: Use chi-squared test for continuous targets
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that performs semantic segmentation on images—classifying each pixel into a category. Which deep learning architecture should they use?
Explanation: U-Net and its variants (like DeepLab, FCN) are designed for semantic segmentation. U-Net uses an encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections—the encoder captures context (what) and the decoder captures localization (where). Each pixel in the output is classified into a category. SageMaker supports custom segmentation models through Hugging Face or custom containers.
Option1: Use U-Net or similar encoder-decoder architecture for pixel-level semantic segmentation
Option2: Use a standard CNN classifier on the whole image
Option3: Use XGBoost on pixel values
Option4: Use K-means clustering on pixel colors

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to handle a multi-label classification problem where each input can belong to multiple categories simultaneously (e.g. a movie can be both 'comedy' and 'romance'). Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Multi-label classification uses sigmoid activation (not softmax) on the output layer, with binary cross-entropy loss for each label independently. Each output neuron predicts the probability of one label, and multiple labels can be active simultaneously. This differs from multi-class classification (softmax, one label per input). The threshold for each label can be tuned independently.
Option1: Use sigmoid activation with binary cross-entropy loss for independent multi-label prediction
Option2: Use softmax activation which forces a single label
Option3: Train separate models for each label
Option4: Use K-means clustering for multi-label classification

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles data versioning for their training datasets so they can reproduce any previous training run. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: S3 versioning combined with SageMaker Experiments tracking provides data versioning. Each training run records the S3 URI (including version ID) of the training data used. SageMaker Processing jobs create versioned datasets with unique S3 prefixes (e.g., s3://bucket/data/v1.2/). This enables exact reproduction of any training run by using the same data version, code version, and hyperparameters.
Option1: Use S3 versioning with SageMaker Experiments to track exact data versions used in each training run
Option2: Overwrite training data in place without versioning
Option3: Keep only the latest version of training data
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that efficiently stores and retrieves high-dimensional feature vectors for similarity search during inference. Which database type should they use?
Explanation: Vector databases (like Amazon OpenSearch with k-NN, Amazon Aurora with pgvector, or Pinecone) are optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors using approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithms. They provide sub-millisecond similarity search across millions of vectors. This is essential for recommendation systems, semantic search, and any ML application requiring similarity-based retrieval.
Option1: Use a vector database like OpenSearch with k-NN for efficient high-dimensional similarity search
Option2: Use a relational database with standard indexes
Option3: Store vectors as JSON in DynamoDB
Option4: Use S3 for vector storage and search

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles data augmentation for their image classification training dataset. The dataset has only 5000 images. Which techniques should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Geometric transformations (rotation, flipping, cropping, scaling) create new training images from existing ones, increasing dataset diversity. Color augmentation (brightness, contrast, saturation, hue adjustments) creates variations that help the model generalize to different lighting conditions. Both increase the effective training set size without collecting new data, reducing overfitting on small datasets.
Option1: Geometric transformations like rotation flipping and cropping
Option2: Color augmentation like brightness contrast and saturation adjustments
Option3: Duplicating images without any transformation
Option4: Reducing image resolution to create more images

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles data from multiple sources with different schemas and formats. Which AWS service provides schema discovery and transformation?
Explanation: AWS Glue Crawlers automatically discover schemas from various data sources (S3, JDBC, DynamoDB) and register them in the Glue Data Catalog. Glue ETL jobs then transform data from different schemas into a unified format. Glue DynamicFrames handle schema inconsistencies (different column types, missing fields) without failing, making them ideal for heterogeneous data sources.
Option1: Use AWS Glue Crawlers for schema discovery and Glue ETL with DynamicFrames for schema unification
Option2: Manually define schemas for each data source
Option3: Require all sources to use the same schema
Option4: Ignore schema differences and concatenate data

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles real-time feature computation for fraud detection. Features include 'average transaction amount in the last 24 hours' and 'number of transactions from this IP in the last hour'. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Kinesis Data Streams ingests transaction events in real-time. Apache Flink (via Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink) computes sliding window aggregations (24-hour average, 1-hour count) in real-time. Computed features are written to SageMaker Feature Store's online store for millisecond-latency retrieval. The fraud detection model reads these real-time features during inference for up-to-date risk scoring.
Option1: Kinesis ingestion with Flink for real-time window aggregations stored in Feature Store online store for inference
Option2: Compute features in batch nightly and use stale values
Option3: Compute all features in the inference endpoint
Option4: Use static features without real-time computation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution that handles data privacy by anonymizing PII in their training dataset. Which TWO techniques should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: K-anonymity ensures each record is indistinguishable from at least K-1 other records by generalizing quasi-identifiers (e.g., replacing exact age with age range). Differential privacy adds calibrated noise to the data or model outputs, providing mathematical guarantees that individual records cannot be identified. Both protect privacy while preserving the statistical properties needed for model training.
Option1: K-anonymity to generalize quasi-identifiers
Option2: Differential privacy to add calibrated noise with mathematical privacy guarantees
Option3: Store PII in plaintext for better model accuracy
Option4: Remove all features to ensure privacy

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to analyze the distribution of their target variable and discovers it follows a Poisson distribution (count data like number of customer complaints per day). Which regression model is appropriate?
Explanation: Poisson regression is designed for count data that follows a Poisson distribution (non-negative integers with a specific mean-variance relationship). Unlike linear regression (which assumes normally distributed continuous targets), Poisson regression models the log of the expected count as a linear function of features. This ensures predictions are non-negative and appropriately models count data.
Option1: Use Poisson regression for count data that follows a Poisson distribution
Option2: Use linear regression for count data
Option3: Use logistic regression for count data
Option4: Use K-means clustering for count prediction

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to determine if two features are statistically independent. Which test should they use for two categorical features?
Explanation: The chi-squared test of independence determines whether there is a statistically significant association between two categorical variables. It compares the observed frequency distribution with the expected distribution under the assumption of independence. A significant result (low p-value) indicates the features are not independent—there is an association between them.
Option1: Use the chi-squared test of independence for two categorical features
Option2: Use Pearson correlation for categorical features
Option3: Use ANOVA for two categorical features
Option4: Use linear regression to test independence

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to handle a dataset where some features have extreme outliers that could affect model training. Which TWO approaches can they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Winsorization caps extreme values at a specified percentile (e.g., values above the 99th percentile are set to the 99th percentile value). This preserves the data point while reducing the outlier's influence. Robust scaling (using median and IQR instead of mean and standard deviation) is less sensitive to outliers than standard scaling. Both handle outliers without removing data points.
Option1: Winsorization to cap extreme values at specified percentiles
Option2: Robust scaling using median and IQR instead of mean and standard deviation
Option3: Remove all data points above the mean
Option4: Ignore outliers entirely

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to perform feature selection to reduce the number of features from 200 to the most informative 50. Which TWO methods can they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) trains a model, ranks features by importance, removes the least important, and repeats until the desired number remains. L1 regularization (Lasso) drives unimportant feature weights to exactly zero, effectively selecting features. Both are systematic approaches to feature selection that identify the most informative features for the target variable.
Option1: Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) for iterative feature ranking and removal
Option2: L1 regularization (Lasso) for automatic feature selection via zero weights
Option3: Select features alphabetically
Option4: Keep all 200 features

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to analyze their dataset for potential bias before training. They want to check if the training data is representative across demographic groups. Which AWS service helps with this?
Explanation: SageMaker Clarify detects bias in training data by computing metrics like Class Imbalance (CI), Difference in Proportions of Labels (DPL), and Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KL) across demographic groups. Pre-training bias metrics identify whether the training data is representative and fair. This analysis should be performed during EDA before model training to address data-level bias early.
Option1: Use SageMaker Clarify pre-training bias metrics to analyze data representativeness across demographic groups
Option2: Assume the training data is unbiased
Option3: Use CloudWatch for bias detection
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that performs sentiment analysis on product reviews. Which approach provides the best accuracy with the least effort?
Explanation: Amazon Comprehend provides managed sentiment analysis without any model training. For custom sentiment categories or domain-specific language, Amazon Comprehend Custom Classification trains a custom model on labeled examples. For maximum accuracy, fine-tuning a pre-trained language model (BERT) on labeled reviews provides state-of-the-art performance. The choice depends on accuracy requirements and available labeled data.
Option1: Use Amazon Comprehend for managed sentiment analysis or fine-tune BERT for maximum custom accuracy
Option2: Use regex patterns to detect sentiment keywords
Option3: Use K-means clustering on review text
Option4: Use linear regression on word counts

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that predicts the probability of an event occurring not just a binary yes/no. Which model output should they use?
Explanation: Logistic regression and neural networks with sigmoid activation output probabilities (0 to 1) rather than hard classifications. The probability represents the model's confidence in the positive class. A threshold (default 0.5) converts probabilities to binary predictions, but the threshold can be adjusted based on the cost of false positives vs. false negatives. Calibrated probabilities are essential for risk scoring.
Option1: Use a model with sigmoid output that produces calibrated probabilities rather than hard classifications
Option2: Use a model that only outputs 0 or 1
Option3: Use regression to predict probabilities above 1
Option4: Use clustering for probability estimation

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that handles sequential data where the order of inputs matters (like text or time series). Which neural network architecture is designed for sequential data?
Explanation: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and their variants—LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) and GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)—are designed for sequential data. They maintain a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps. Transformers (with self-attention) have largely replaced RNNs for many sequence tasks due to better parallelization and long-range dependency handling.
Option1: Use LSTM/GRU for sequential data or Transformers for better long-range dependency handling
Option2: Use standard feedforward neural networks for sequences
Option3: Use K-means clustering for sequential data
Option4: Use PCA for sequence modeling

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement ensemble learning to improve model performance. Which TWO ensemble techniques should they consider? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) trains multiple models on random subsets of the data and averages their predictions—Random Forest is a bagging ensemble of decision trees. Boosting trains models sequentially, each focusing on the errors of the previous model—XGBoost and AdaBoost are boosting ensembles. Both reduce variance (bagging) or bias (boosting) compared to single models.
Option1: Bagging like Random Forest for variance reduction
Option2: Boosting like XGBoost for bias reduction
Option3: Training a single large model
Option4: Using only the simplest model available

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that performs image classification with the highest possible accuracy on a custom dataset. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Transfer learning from a pre-trained model (ResNet, EfficientNet, or Vision Transformer trained on ImageNet) provides the best accuracy on custom datasets. The pre-trained model has learned general image features (edges, textures, shapes) from millions of images. Fine-tuning the last few layers on the custom dataset adapts these features to the specific classification task with much less data than training from scratch.
Option1: Use transfer learning from a pre-trained ImageNet model and fine-tune on the custom dataset
Option2: Train a CNN from scratch on the custom dataset
Option3: Use XGBoost on raw pixel values
Option4: Use K-means clustering on image features

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to choose a learning rate for training their neural network. Which approach finds the optimal learning rate?
Explanation: Learning rate finder (popularized by fast.ai) trains the model for a few iterations with exponentially increasing learning rates, plotting loss vs. learning rate. The optimal learning rate is just before the loss starts increasing rapidly. This empirical approach finds the sweet spot—too low causes slow convergence, too high causes divergence. Learning rate schedulers then adjust the rate during training.
Option1: Use a learning rate finder to empirically determine the optimal learning rate
Option2: Always use a learning rate of 0.01
Option3: Use the largest possible learning rate
Option4: Use a learning rate of 0 for stability

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to build a model that detects objects in video frames in real-time. Which deep learning architecture is optimized for real-time object detection?
Explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) processes the entire image in a single forward pass, making it extremely fast for real-time detection. Unlike two-stage detectors (like Faster R-CNN) that first propose regions then classify them, YOLO predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities simultaneously. YOLOv5/v8 achieves a good balance of speed and accuracy for real-time video applications.
Option1: Use YOLO for real-time object detection optimized for speed
Option2: Use Faster R-CNN which is slower but more accurate
Option3: Use image classification on each frame
Option4: Use K-means on pixel values for detection

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model endpoint handles multiple model variants for different customer segments. Which SageMaker feature supports this?
Explanation: SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints (MME) host multiple models on a single endpoint, dynamically loading and unloading models based on traffic patterns. Each request specifies which model to invoke via the TargetModel parameter. This is cost-effective when you have many models (one per customer segment) with sporadic traffic—models share the same infrastructure instead of requiring separate endpoints.
Option1: Use SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints to host multiple models on a single endpoint
Option2: Deploy a separate endpoint for each customer segment
Option3: Use a single model for all segments
Option4: Use Lambda for multi-model serving

QuestionText: A machine learning engineer needs to implement a solution where their model training pipeline includes automated data validation to catch data quality issues before training begins. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: SageMaker Processing jobs can run data validation scripts that check for data quality issues: schema validation (correct columns and types), statistical validation (feature distributions match expectations), completeness checks (missing value percentages within thresholds), and freshness checks (data is recent enough). The pipeline fails if validation fails, preventing training on bad data.
Option1: Use SageMaker Processing jobs for automated data validation as the first pipeline step
Option2: Skip validation and train on whatever data is available
Option3: Validate data manually before each training run
Option4: Validate data only after model deployment
